revision.text:
[[ja:秋田県護国神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Akitaken Gokoku Jinja haiden 20190113.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|39.74113888888889|N|140.07919444444445|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Akita Prefectural Gokoku Shrine | caption = | map_type = Japan Akita | location = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|qid=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}} {{ill|Akita (city)|en|Akita (city)|qid=Q17139|lt=Akita City|1=Akita (city)}} Terauchi Ōbatake 5-3 | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service
{{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}}
{{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉尊|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q682306}} | type = Minister of the Interior-designated {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}, {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} | established = {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 2 ({{ill|1869|en|1869|qid=Q7720|lt=Year 1869|1=1869}}) | festival = {{ill|April 29|en|April 29|qid=Q2535|lt=April 29|1=April 29}}・{{ill|October 24|en|October 24|qid=Q2955|lt=October 24|1=October 24}} | prefecture= {{ill|Akita|qid=Q17139|1=Akita (city)}} }} {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社|1=Akita Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (秋田県護国神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!|qq=}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com|qq=}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?|qq=}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|qid=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X|qq=}} '''Akita Prefectural Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|秋田県護国神社|qq=}}, Akita-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}) located in {{ill|Akita (city)|en|Akita (city)|qid=Q17139|lt=Akita City|1=Akita (city)}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|qid=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}. It enshrines those from Akita Prefecture who died in war, as well as {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊弉諾尊|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊弉冉尊|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q682306}}. == History == In Meiji 2 (1869), {{ill|Satake Yoshitaka|en|Satake Yoshitaka|qid=Q8514549|lt=Satake Yoshitaka|1=Satake Yoshitaka}}, lord of the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|lt=Akita Domain|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1067720}}, enshrined 425 fallen imperial soldiers of the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|qid=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} on Takashimizu Hill, marking the shrine’s origin. It was destroyed by fire in Meiji 26 (1893), but rebuilt in Meiji 32 (1899) in Senshū Park (the site of {{ill|Kubota Castle|en|Kubota Castle|qid=Q6133648|lt=Kubota Castle|1=Kubota Castle}}) in Akita City. It was then called "Akita Shōkonsha" (Akita Shōkon Shrine) and enshrined soldiers and military personnel from Akita Prefecture. In Shōwa 14 (1939), it was designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of the Interior and renamed "Akita Prefectural Gokoku Shrine." The following year, it was relocated to its current location, the site of the old {{ill|Akita Castle|en|Akita Castle|qid=Q11595283|lt=Akita Castle|1=Akita Castle}}. After World War II, when there was a risk of it being abolished as a "militarist facility," it temporarily took the name "Takashimizu-gū," enshrining Izanagi and Izanami as its main deities to safeguard its existence. Following the war, it continued to enshrine the spirits of military personnel and support staff related to the prefecture. Currently, most war dead have been enshrined, bringing the total number of enshrined spirits to over 38,000. On July 9, Heisei 2 (1990), during the time of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremony]], the shrine’s buildings were targeted in a terrorist attack by the {{ill|Revolutionary Communist League, National Committee|en|Revolutionary Communist League, National Committee|qid=Q2972178|lt=Revolutionary Communist League, National Committee|1=Revolutionary Communist League, National Committee}} opposing the enthronement. Two time bombs were placed in the shrine, resulting in a fire that completely destroyed the buildings. (It is believed that the shrine was targeted because a sacred field for the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|qid=Q11433653|lt=Daijōsai ceremony|1=Daijosai}} had been chosen in Akita Prefecture.) However, the shintai (sacred object) and reiji-bō (register of the enshrined spirits) stored within the main hall remained unharmed. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in Heisei 4 (1992). == Festivals == The major festivals are held twice a year: in spring on {{ill|April 29|en|April 29|qid=Q2535|lt=April 29|1=April 29}}, and in autumn on {{ill|October 24|en|October 24|qid=Q2955|lt=October 24|1=October 24}}. == Timeline == * Meiji 2 (1869): Satake Yoshitaka enshrines 425 imperial soldiers who fell in the Boshin War. * Meiji 26 (1893): The shrine is destroyed by fire. * Meiji 32 (1899): Rebuilt in Senshū Park, Akita City, and named "Akita Shōkonsha." * Shōwa 14 (1939): Designated a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of the Interior and renamed "Akita Prefectural Gokoku Shrine." * Shōwa 15 (1940): Relocated to the former Akita Castle site. * Shōwa 44 (1969), August 26: On the occasion of the visit to Akita by {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Emperor Shōwa|1=Hirohito}} and {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|qid=Q230697|lt=Empress Kōjun|1=Empress Nagako}}, the shrine was among the places visited.{{Cite book |author=Hara Takeshi |title=Shōwa Tennō Gosho Ressha Zen Kiroku (Complete Records of Emperor Shōwa’s Imperial Train) |publisher=Shinchosha |year=2016-09-30 |page=134 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4|qq=}} * Heisei 2 (1990): The shrine buildings are destroyed by a terrorist attack by radical leftists opposing the Enthronement Ceremony. * Heisei 4 (1992): Rebuilt on the current site. == Access == === Rail === * Nearest station: {{ill|Tsuchizaki Station|en|Tsuchizaki Station|qid=Q7849925|lt=Tsuchizaki Station|1=Tsuchizaki Station}} === Bus === * "Gokoku Jinja Iriguchi" or "Gokoku Jinja Ura Sandō" bus stops (served by lines from {{ill|Akita Station|en|Akita Station|qid=Q1316396|lt=Akita Station|1=Akita Station}} on Shōgunno Line, Terauchi/Tsuchizaki routes, etc.) ** {{ill|Akita Chūō Kōtsū|ja|秋田中央交通|lt=Akita Chūō Kōtsū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Chūō Kōtsū|1=Akita Chūō Kōtsū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11595235}} === Other === * Parking available. == Other == * The shrine is famous for its cherry blossoms, which typically bloom around the spring festival. * == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|qid=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|qid=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|qid=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|qid=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|qid=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|qid=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|qid=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|qid=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|qid=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|qid=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|qid=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|qid=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|qid=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|qid=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} == Notes == {{Notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == References == * Akita Sakigake Shinpo-sha (ed.) "Akita Daijiten" (Akita Encyclopedia), Akita Sakigake Shinpo-sha, 1981, ISBN 4870200074 *[https://web.archive.org/web/20040608192019/http://akitajinja33.hp.infoseek.co.jp/index.htm Chinju no Mori Akita] {{Dead link|date=October 2019|qq=}} {{commonscat|Akitaken-Gokoku-jinja|qq=}} }} {{shinto-stub|qq=}} {{Beppyo Shrines|qq=}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|秋田県護国神社|version=101986417|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines|qq=}} {{everybodywiki link|Akita Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:秋田県護国神社]] [[ja:Akita Gokoku Shrine]] {{Commons category|Akitaken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11595542}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Akita (city)]] [[Category:秋田市の歴史]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]
